name: Kailua-Kona Coast
description:
The Kona Coast of Hawaii's Big Island is one of the world's premier big-game fishing destinations. The deep water drops off steeply just offshore, putting anglers over thousands of feet of water within minutes of leaving the harbor.

Blue marlin exceeding 1,000 pounds — known as granders — are caught here more often than anywhere else on Earth. The annual Hawaiian International Billfish Tournament draws elite anglers from around the globe. Yellowfin tuna, mahi-mahi, and wahoo provide consistent action year-round. Honokohau Harbor is the primary launch and charter base.

name: Oahu North Shore
description:
Oahu's North Shore is famous for surfing, but the offshore waters produce world-class fishing. Haleiwa Small Boat Harbor provides the primary launch point for boats heading into deep Pacific waters.

Yellowfin tuna are caught year-round, with the best fishing from May through September. Giant trevally (ulua) are prized shore and nearshore targets, with fish exceeding 100 pounds. The North Shore's exposure to open ocean swells creates nutrient-rich upwellings that attract pelagic species close to shore.

name: Maui Coast
description:
Maui's coastline offers excellent fishing from both Lahaina Harbor on the west side and Maalaea Harbor in the center. The channels between Maui, Molokai, and Lanai concentrate baitfish and attract large pelagic predators.

The Auau Channel between Maui and Lanai is particularly productive for marlin and tuna. Mahi-mahi fishing is outstanding during spring and summer months. Shore-based ulua (giant trevally) fishing is a cultural tradition, with dedicated anglers targeting these powerful fish from rocky coastlines. Both harbors have boat launch facilities.

name: Kaneohe Bay
description:
Kaneohe Bay is the largest sheltered body of water in Hawaii, located on Oahu's windward coast. The bay's extensive reef system, sand flats, and seagrass beds create ideal habitat for nearshore species.

Bonefish (oio) are the premier fly-fishing target, with Kaneohe Bay offering some of the best bonefishing in the main Hawaiian islands. Small trevally (papio) provide exciting light-tackle action around the reef edges. The bay's calm, protected waters make it ideal for kayak fishing. Heeia Kea Boat Harbor provides the primary launch access.

name: Pearl Harbor
description:
Pearl Harbor, beyond its historical significance, offers accessible fishing opportunities in its outer channels and adjacent shoreline areas. Civilian fishing access is limited but available in designated areas around the harbor complex.

Small trevally and barracuda are common catches around structure and channel edges. Shore fishing along the harbor's accessible areas produces mullet, tilapia, and occasional bonefish on the flats. The nearby Keehi Lagoon boat ramp provides the closest public launch access for boats heading to surrounding waters.

name: Wahiawa Reservoir
description:
Wahiawa Reservoir, also known as Lake Wilson, is Oahu's largest freshwater body and one of Hawaii's best freshwater fishing destinations. The lake sits in central Oahu's agricultural plateau and offers a unique tropical freshwater fishery.

Peacock bass (tucunare) are the star attraction — introduced from South America, these colorful, aggressive fish thrive in the warm water and can exceed 5 pounds. Largemouth bass also do well, and channel catfish provide bottom-fishing action. A public boat ramp on the east shore provides access, with electric motors preferred.
